title: Personal Data Protection Code of Practice (Health) 2022
summary: This sector-specific Code applies to all data users in the Health class (Class 4 of the Class of Data Users Order 2013), including private hospitals, specialist centres, clinics, pharmacies, diagnostic laboratories, and other registered healthcare providers. Health data is sensitive personal data under s29 of the PDPA. This Code imposes enhanced obligations commensurate with the sensitivity of medical information and the significant harm that can result from its unauthorized disclosure. The Code takes into account requirements under the Private Healthcare Facilities and Services Act 1998, the Medical Act 1971, and professional ethics obligations.

provisions.title: Scope and Application
provisions.content: This Code applies to all Class 4 data users including private hospitals, specialist clinics, general practice clinics, dental practices, pharmacies, diagnostic laboratories, physiotherapy centres, and traditional and complementary medicine (TCM) practitioners registered under applicable health legislation. All health data is sensitive personal data under s29 of the PDPA. Health data users must comply with both this Code and all applicable professional ethics and confidentiality requirements under health profession legislation.
requirements: All patient data is sensitive personal data by definition — no case-by-case assessment needed
Professional ethics obligations (doctor-patient confidentiality) apply alongside PDPA
Telehealth providers are subject to this Code for patient data processed in connection with Malaysian patients
This Code must be read together with the General Code 2022

provisions.title: Patient Consent for Health Data Processing
provisions.content: Healthcare providers must obtain patient consent for the processing of health data in a manner that is: (a) specific to the purpose of processing (treatment, billing, insurance, research are each separate purposes); (b) documented, typically at the point of registration and updated when new purposes arise; (c) granular — separate consent for treatment, billing, insurance disclosure, and research; (d) meaningful — patients must understand what they are consenting to, in language and level of detail appropriate to them; and (e) revocable — patients must be able to withdraw consent for non-essential processing (e.g., research, marketing) without affecting their right to receive treatment. Where the patient is unable to give consent (emergency, incapacity), the vital interests exception applies.
requirements: Consent at registration must cover treatment, billing, and insurance as standard
Research consent must be separate and specific to each research project
Emergency processing under vital interests must be documented
Minor patient consent requires parental/guardian consent for patients under 18

provisions.title: Electronic Health Records (EHR) Security
provisions.content: Healthcare providers maintaining electronic health records must implement security measures including: (a) role-based access controls limiting EHR access to treating clinicians and authorized support staff; (b) encryption of all EHR data at rest (AES-256 minimum) and in transit (TLS 1.2+); (c) comprehensive audit logging of all access to and modifications of patient records; (d) two-factor authentication for EHR system access; (e) automatic logout after 10 minutes of inactivity; (f) no patient data on unencrypted portable devices; and (g) annual security assessments of EHR systems including penetration testing. Emergency access provisions must be documented and subject to post-access review.
requirements: EHR audit logs must capture: user, date/time, patient record accessed, and action taken
EHR audit logs retained for minimum 3 years
Emergency (break-glass) access must trigger automatic alert to the Privacy Officer
Penetration testing of EHR systems annually

provisions.title: Disclosure to Insurers and Third Parties
provisions.content: Healthcare providers must obtain patient consent before disclosing health data to: (a) insurance companies and managed care organizations (MCOs) for claims purposes; (b) employers for occupational health assessments; (c) other healthcare providers outside the immediate treating team; (d) legal representatives in connection with litigation; and (e) researchers and academic institutions. Mandatory disclosures to public health authorities (Ministry of Health, state health departments) are governed by the Prevention and Control of Infectious Diseases Act 1988 and do not require patient consent. Disclosures to law enforcement require lawful authority.
requirements: Insurance disclosure consent must be specific to the claim — blanket consent is insufficient
Employer occupational health disclosure requires specific consent for each disclosed item
Referring to another provider is within the treating purpose — no additional consent needed
Research disclosures require research-specific consent or ethics board approved anonymization

provisions.title: Medical Records Retention
provisions.content: Healthcare providers must retain medical records for the following minimum periods: (a) adult patient records: 7 years from the date of last treatment or consultation; (b) minor patient records: until the patient reaches the age of 25 or 7 years from the date of last treatment, whichever is later; (c) surgical records and operation notes: 10 years; (d) X-rays and medical imaging: 5 years from date of examination; (e) obstetric records: 25 years; and (f) records relating to deceased patients: 7 years from date of death. After expiry of the retention period, records must be securely destroyed with a documented disposal certificate.
requirements: Minimum retention periods are mandatory — premature disposal is a breach
Patient records may be retained longer where legal proceedings are ongoing
Disposal must produce a certificate — not simply deleting the EHR record
Legacy paper records converting to electronic format must be handled under appropriate security

provisions.title: Telehealth and Digital Health
provisions.content: Healthcare providers offering telehealth services must: (a) ensure that video consultations are conducted over encrypted platforms meeting minimum security standards; (b) not record telehealth consultations without explicit patient consent for the recording; (c) treat telehealth consultation notes as equivalent to in-person medical records; (d) ensure that patient data submitted through patient-facing applications (appointment booking, symptom checkers, e-prescriptions) is processed in accordance with this Code; and (e) conduct DPIAs for new telehealth platforms before deployment. Where telehealth providers are located outside Malaysia, cross-border transfer requirements under s129A must be complied with.
requirements: Telehealth platforms must use end-to-end encrypted video conferencing
Consultation recording consent must be specific and documented
DPIA required before deploying new telehealth platforms
Overseas telehealth service providers must have s129A-compliant transfer mechanisms

provisions.title: Medical Research and Secondary Use
provisions.content: Healthcare providers using patient data for medical research or allowing third parties to access patient data for research must: (a) obtain specific research consent from patients, or ensure the research is conducted on genuinely anonymized or pseudonymized data; (b) obtain ethics committee approval for research involving patient data; (c) implement data minimization — provide only the minimum data necessary for the research; (d) execute data sharing agreements with research institutions; and (e) not re-identify pseudonymized research data. Secondary uses of patient data for quality improvement, audit, and healthcare management within the organization are permitted without additional consent but must be disclosed in the privacy notice.
requirements: Research consent must be specific to each research project
Ethics committee approval is a prerequisite for any research using patient data
Data sharing agreements with researchers must include restrictions on re-identification
True anonymization (irreversible) removes data from PDPA scope; pseudonymization does not

provisions.title: Patient Rights in the Healthcare Context
provisions.content: Healthcare providers must facilitate the exercise of patient data subject rights, including: (a) right of access — patients are entitled to a copy of their medical records; healthcare providers may charge a reasonable copying fee but must not obstruct access; (b) right of correction — inaccurate clinical records must be corrected, but a healthcare provider may document the basis for declining a correction where clinical judgment supports the original record; (c) opt-out from non-essential processing — patients may opt out of research, marketing, and other non-treatment processing; and (d) right to complain — the Commissioner must be identified in the privacy notice as the body to which patients may complain. Healthcare providers must not refuse treatment as a condition of data subject rights compliance.
requirements: Medical records access must not be made practically difficult
Copying fee for access requests must not exceed the prescribed amount
Clinical correction refusals must be documented with clinical reasons
Treatment must never be made conditional on waiving data subject rights
